mission.title: Install and Configure Redis Operator (Spotahome / OT-CONTAINER-KIT)
mission.description: The Redis Operator by OT-CONTAINER-KIT simplifies the deployment and management of Redis instances on Kubernetes, supporting standalone, cluster, and sentinel modes. It provides built-in monitoring and adheres to best practices for security and performance. This operator is ideal for teams looking to efficiently manage Redis workloads in a Kubernetes environment.

steps.title: Add the Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official Redis Operator Helm chart repository to your local Helm configuration.

```bash

helm repo add redis-operator https://opstree.github.io/redis-operator

```

> This command adds the repository where the Redis Operator Helm chart is hosted.

steps.title: Install the Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs)
steps.description:
CRDs are necessary for the operator to manage Redis resources effectively.

```bash

kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/OT-CONTAINER-KIT/redis-operator/v0.24.0/deploy/crds/redis.opstree.dev_redisclusters_crd.yaml

```

> This command installs the required CRDs for the Redis Operator.

steps.title: Install the Redis Operator
steps.description:
This step installs the Redis Operator using Helm, which will manage Redis instances in your Kubernetes cluster.

```bash

helm install redis-operator redis-operator/redis-operator --namespace redis-operator --create-namespace --version 0.24.0

```

> This command deploys the Redis Operator in the 'redis-operator' namespace.

steps.title: Create a Redis Cluster Custom Resource
steps.description:
This step creates a Redis cluster by applying a custom resource definition that the operator will manage.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: redis.opstree.dev/v1
kind: RedisCluster
metadata:
  name: my-redis-cluster
  namespace: redis-operator
spec:
  size: 3
  redis:
    image: redis:7.0.0
EOF

```

> This command creates a Redis cluster named 'my-redis-cluster' with 3 replicas.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: redis.opstree.dev/v1
kind: RedisCluster
metadata:
  name: my-redis-cluster
  namespace: redis-operator
spec:
  size: 3
  redis:
    image: redis:7.0.0
EOF

steps.title: Verify the Redis Cluster
steps.description:
This step checks the status of the Redis cluster to ensure it is running correctly.

```bash

kubectl get redisclusters -n redis-operator

```

> This command lists the Redis clusters in the 'redis-operator' namespace.

uninstall.title: Uninstall the Redis Operator
uninstall.description:
This step removes the Redis Operator and all associated resources. Be cautious as this may lead to data loss.

```bash

helm uninstall redis-operator --namespace redis-operator

kubectl delete namespace redis-operator

```

> This command uninstalls the Redis Operator from the specified namespace.
> This command deletes the namespace and all resources within it.

upgrade.title: Upgrade the Redis Operator
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you back up your data. This step will upgrade the Redis Operator to the latest version.

```bash

kubectl get redisclusters -n redis-operator -o yaml > backup-redisclusters.yaml

helm upgrade redis-operator redis-operator/redis-operator --namespace redis-operator --version 0.24.0

kubectl get pods -n redis-operator

```

> Backup existing Redis cluster configurations.
> This command upgrades the Redis Operator to the specified version.
> Verify that the Redis Operator pods are running after the upgrade.
commands: kubectl get redisclusters -n redis-operator -o yaml > backup-redisclusters.yaml

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to create Redis cluster'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The Redis Operator may not have sufficient permissions.

**Fix:**
Ensure that the operator has the necessary RBAC permissions configured.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Redis cluster is not ready'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** One or more Redis pods may be in a crash loop.

**Fix:**
Check the logs of the Redis pods using 'kubectl logs <pod-name> -n redis-operator' to diagnose the issue.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'No resources found'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The Redis cluster may not have been created successfully.

**Fix:**
Verify the custom resource definition and ensure it was applied correctly.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'ImagePullBackOff'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The specified Redis image may not be available.

**Fix:**
Check the image name and tag in the RedisCluster spec and ensure it is correct.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for Redis 7.0, improved monitoring capabilities with redis-exporter.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: No specific migration steps required for this version.

resolution.summary: You should see the Redis cluster listed with 'kubectl get redisclusters -n redis-operator' and its status should be 'Ready'.
codeSnippets:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: redis.opstree.dev/v1
kind: RedisCluster
metadata:
  name: my-redis-cluster
  namespace: redis-operator
spec:
  size: 3
  redis:
    image: redis:7.0.0
EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with the necessary tools installed.
